The first thing you figure out is, that there's not the same built in functions on both sides.
It sounds like a very easy translation, but you have to be careful, for instance when it comes to decimals.
The built-in TO_NUMBER() function rounds at the 16th decimal place and if you use CONVERT, the behavior is a bit different.
This function is well know by the Oracle optimizer which makes this function unbelievable fast and there is no similar function on SQL Server
I had good performance measures on an easy simple query but invinitive response with more complex
In my case it solve the performance issue from not responding to 1-5 second each view
I stored this complex query to a temp table and queried recursively on this temp-table. With this the complexity for the recursive query disappeared
When I implemented all about 20 views from Oracle to SQL Server, I had to test the performance and not least important, the content of the views if their still contain the same.
Don't forget to clear the cache before each run.
If you have only a few comparison you easily create EXCEPT-SELECT-Query and compare your Oracle Views over the database link to your Microsoft Views.

People will appreciate that you share something they can use without asking for something in return. AND, later on, they will remember you when they find something that might be of interest to you!
Maybe even more valuable than this, are the discussions that arise from sharing, which will inevitably lead to many new ideas.
It's no surprise that open source software or open source, in general, is more popular than ever before as it is all about sharing and learning from each other.

Very long time ago, 1986, 31 years ago to be precise, IBM in Europe created the very first architecture of a data warehouse. And it seems to be a masterpiece as it didn't change much since.
No matter how we want to improve the architecture, it has to be automated as much as possible.
Nowadays it became too slow for serving the business needs doing it the traditional way.
I strongly believe that DWA tools are the future and will boost the Data Warehousing reputation back to earlier years.
I don't think DWH will go away anytime soon.
Every one needs to make data driven decision faster, why not use a generator which gives you answers in days instead of months..?
If you don't trust in your employees why then even hire them in the first place?
You can achieve with existing resources more in the same time and don't even have to hire new employees
Data Warehouse Automation tools are here to automate the boring tasks and leave the fun stuff for the developer
I don't like having an emotional discussion, emotions only blur the picture. In my opinion, it should always be based on facts
The business wants to see results faster and faster also because they get used to the way of working with data lakes where data can be added immediately

As a developer, I'm looking forward to learn new exciting tools, deliver significantly faster, with higher consistency and leave the boring tasks to the tool. I will go for the potential and not fight against it.
In a society where time flys remarkably fast and data became the new gold, it's crucial to have proper analyses or even data predictive trends to navigate the company in the best direction.
It's not the time to do recurring tasks manually over again and producing additional errors.
DWA enables you to concentrate on examining the data not hunting and building them
Testing can be automated (which is almost impossible doing it in the traditional way)
We do the same thing over and over again in Data Warehousing.
It is actually hard to find a reason not to automate.
Don't waste more time and work smart at the speed of business with DWA!
You quickly generate a model with the data which is available
The benefit is you only work with data that is actually there and the business has already a real-life model that represents the data
This suits very well with the agile methodology of Data Warehouse Automation tools

Other vendors are not transparent and hand over prices on request

It is not Data Warehouses that are dead, but the traditional way of designing and building them.
More data is always better but can also lead to more chaotic data stores where nobody knows what's in there.
The Data Warehouse is designed for slowly changing data; Data Lakes are designed for quickly changing data.

Python is here to stay and it's becoming the Swiss Army Knife of programming languages.

It's ineffective because it's based on old fashion expectations. We approach email like a letter, where every email will be answered immediately the moment the letter arrives.
Without email, our informants focused longer on their tasks, multitasked less, and had lower stress.

My goal was to own my notes as it's my data and my knowledge.
I didn't want to migrate from one proprietary format into the next one to lock me in yet another time.
Obsidian is just a wrapper around your folders and Markdown files, including one single folder called .obsidian in your root. That's it!
As everything is now plain text, I can search, replace or write a small Python script to fix these things later on.
In case you want to open all notes in one go, use open Obsidian.

Instead of defining the metrics such as distinctUsers in various places, we store it once and apply it to all systems. No need to worry if you got the latest version or if anyone changed the calculation.
See it as certain business logic, critical code or metadata implemented once centrally following the DRY principles.
Despite how far we came with all data tools and frameworks, people are still running around and matching up correct numbers, and fighting who's once are correct.

A Data Orchestrator models dependencies between different tasks in heterogeneous environments end-to-end.
In the end, all data must come from somewhere and go somewhere. Modern data orchestrators are the layer that interconnects all those tools, data, practitioners, and stakeholders.
A data asset is typically a database table, a machine learning model, or a report – a persistent object that captures some understanding of the world.
Similar to how DevOps changed how software gets deployed with Kubernetes and descriptive YAML, the exact same should happen with data pipelines.
Declarative approaches appeal because they make systems easier to debug and automate.
Abstractions let you use data pipelines as a microservice on steroids.
We have seen the same story over and over again. Products start small, in time, add adjacent verticals and functionality to their offerings, and become a platform.
A data lake also removes the need to go through a proprietary format that traditional BI tools had done to transform the data.
With a data lake, data becomes increasingly available, and early adopters discovered that they could extract insight through new applications built to serve the business.
Imagine inserting into hundreds of files at once. They are the key and usually relay on one of the above, open-source data lake file formats that optimize columnar storage and are highly compressed.
These data lake table formats are the engine of the data lake file format.

Unified batch and streaming mean the Lambda Architecture is obsolete. No need to differentiate your data architecture in batch and streaming—they end both in the same tables with less complexity and more speed.

A lakehouse is more open (open-formats) and more difficult as more DIY and patching different tools together, supporting more ML/DS/AI use cases whereas a data warehouse is more closed (mostly closed-source), build for BI, fully managed, and more expensive to scale.
There is a big run for data lake table formats; every big vendor is either having one themself or searching for the perfect open-source one.
Instead of re-creating siloed metrics in each app, we want to define them once, open in a version-controlled way and sync them into each visualization tool.
Because everyone has different definitions of 'active' users or 'paying' customers, the semantic layer allows you to define these discrepancies once company-wide.
A semantic layer transforms/joins data at query time, whereas the transformation layer does during the transform (T) step of ETL/ELT, meaning it's pre-calculated.

Suppose we would push all metric logic into the transformation layer, with dbt, for example. In that case, we'd lose this agility and store multiple versions of each table with different granularities.
With the fast-rising complexity of data and its ecosystem, you also need better abstractions!
The cost of maintaining and creating such a layer is high.
It's still easier to maintain copies of data sets and re-do the metrics repetitively inside a BI dashboard.
Will Rust kill Python for Data Engineers? If you only came here to know this, my answer is no. Betteridge's Law strikes again!
Was Python made for Data Engineering in the first place?
When you write any code, the goal is that the code doesn't break during the weekend or at night when you sleep.

Rust's developer experience goes much further than just offering a language specification and a compiler; many aspects of creating and maintaining production-quality software are treated as first-class citizens.
It's pretty hard to imagine that inertia changing substantially in the next decade.
What was once supposed to be Scala will now be Rust—a backend tooling language to do tasks that need fast and well-maintained code, including a Python wrapper on top.
Writing libraries in Rust feels more like writing long-term infrastructure than writing in higher-level languages such as Python, Java, or the JVM.
These features open instrumental use cases for data integration as code.
What now if you are dependent on incoming metadata that can change? If the configs are not hard coded, you'd need a script on top to generate the configurations dynamically.
Imagine you need to provision Airbyte, have multi-tenancy requirements for teams or customers, or read from a dynamic API.
Based on these configs, you can automatically apply new sync based on the latest status. Everything is versioned, which leads to changes with confidence.
The Pythonic definition of all Airbyte components opened the pandora box for more event-based use cases orchestrated by Dagster.
Software-Defined Asset in Dagster treats each of our destination tables from Airbyte as a Data Product.
The Open Data Stack is a better term for the Modern Data Stack, but focusing on solutions built on open source and open standards covering the data engineering lifecycle.
With the open data stack, companies can reuse existing battle-tested solutions and build on top of them instead of having to reinvent the wheel.
Every data project starts with some SQL queries. The default should be to use dbt, which immediately forces you to use the best software engineering practices.
Write business logic with just a SQL select statement or a Python DataFrame that returns the dataset you need, and dbt takes care of materialization.
As data pipelines quickly grow in complexity and data teams grow in numbers, using methodologies that provide clarity isn't a luxury—it's a necessity.
The beauty of the data stack is that you can now add specific use cases with other tools and frameworks.
I've since learned that Vim is the only editor that you get faster using with time.
Everyone who types on a computer eight hours a day should learn the Vim language.
Vim is the only editor that optimizes editing text instead of writing from a blank page.
With a couple of verbs and objects, you can already know hundreds of combinations without memorizing each one individually.
It felt liberating, moving my cursor with the precision of a surgeon.
An underrated skill in general among programmers is using the terminal.
Vim changed not only my workflow but how I was able to edit at the speed of thought.
Working in Neovim is one of the highlights of my everyday work.
My colleagues and I have essentially re-implemented the MDS repeatedly at each company in one way or another.

Today you get the full data stack for free, open-sourced on GitHub. It's a game-changer and is one reason that small companies can outperform large enterprises regularly with strong data-driven strategies.
Working with data within a big organization is complicated and unpredictable. On the surface, it might not look that challenging.
Integrating good tools doesn't mean you'll get a good stack and expected results. It's still a complex thing.
Large enterprises typically ignore the trends until they are unavoidable.
Your final choices have long-term consequences, as you lock the company into that proprietary tool.
Ask yourself: 'Do we have the capacity and knowledge to own and manage the data stack ourselves?'
I have learned that some of my best decision-making comes from following my gut, heart, and intuition - a place of inner knowing.
The Pathless Path is experimenting on your own to find a life you want to live vs 'getting ahead' on a script others follow.
In these 'stress' situations, you get to know yourself and deeply engage with the surroundings and people around you.
Being a minimalist, I make impactful decisions. Saying no to things I didn't value meant I could follow my passions.
Ultimately, it is anyone's path; This was my Pathless Path, and it was, in a sense, genuinely pathless—following curiosity and my instincts.
Try to be happy with less. Try minimalism. Then, happiness = reality - expectations.
Finding love is likely to happen when you are your best self. Not when stuck in a daily commute or working 9-5.
Apache Arrow allows sharing data in an in-memory representation without persisting.
Zero-copy data access, mapping complex tables to memory to make accessing one terabyte of data on disk as fast and easy as one megabyte.
If you do not need to persist the data set at each step, that's a tremendous boost in performance and cost.
The bulk of the data, GBs or TBs, must not be serialized into another format. This allows data sharing easily, even with 'big data'.
Polars is the more accessible version of Spark and easier to understand than Pandas.
Whatever tool you use, once Pandas 2.0 is used, if you use Arrow's types, converting between Pandas, Polars, and others will be almost immediate.
Most data projects initially ignore data modeling because of time constraints, fast-paced development, or sometimes lack of knowledge.

Instead of investing time into sketching out how the data should flow from its source, the organization's requirements, to how the end user wants to analyze its data, projects get built quickly and with a 'dirty' MVP.
Due to a lack of time and resources, these small teams get pressured to deliver a valuable product to the business immediately after showing a working MVP.
If you do not know about Big-O notation, you are probably not delivering on good performant code.
If we see data engineering through the lens of data modeling, I am optimistic that we have an angle to work against these forces and keep the complexity of storing data low.
People have realized that a solid data architecture and choice of tools matter a lot regarding the success of a data-driven culture and the cost of using data.
Instead of thinking in your small garden ahead, you zoom out and see the whole field.

Data literacy is the ability to derive meaningful information from data, just as literacy, in general, is the ability to derive information from the written word—or, said differently, extrapolating the business value from the data given.
Is dimensional modeling still needed within data engineering compared to its popularity way back? The answer is a resounding yes—perhaps even more so than before.
By incorporating a robust dimensional model at the core of every data project, data engineers are compelled to consider critical questions related to granularity, entities, metrics, and more.
Data modeling is easy to neglect; assessing the consequences can take time and effort.
If you initially ignore poor data modeling and architecture decisions, you'll likely notice problems in the last mile, thinking they might be due to the tools or insights.

The benefit of a conceptual model in a larger enterprise organization of sufficient complexity gives you a great framework to order the logical and physical models around, decoupled from the source systems.
Isn't it amazing that something from 2016 is still so current? That's why data modeling is getting into vogue again, as it has never been entirely outdated.
Still, to this day, streaming is primarily optional. Suppose you tell the business team you can achieve near real-time with hourly batching; they will be happy.
As smart as ChatGPT might be, it would need to understand the semantics of how these IDs coalesce into something meaningful to automate any step of modeling or ETL.
If there is a place where you do not want to use automation, then it would be in data modeling and the overall data architecture.
A data engineer will always be needed to assess the outcome, understand and make sense of the data in front of him and understand the overall data flow of the organization.
Whatever you choose, start with simplicity. You can always add complexity later when the product and solution mature.
The essence of data modeling is simplicity, no matter how complex the underlying data might be.
That's how a surgeon would work: fine-grained and filigree, but with words.
I can edit my second brain, and text I write everywhere with the speed of thought.
It felt powerful. I had one version of the truth. No more formatting battles, no more fear I'd lose a bolding text or a code formatting.
It was like learning a game, adding a new superpower (shortcut) every week.

It's not about which tool you use, as you can achieve the same with any other.
Even after Obsidian might die one day, as they are just simple text files with Markdown, they can be opened by any text editor in the past and future.
I do not need to spend a thought on where to store my note as one note can potentially belong to many different areas of my life.

The satisfaction I get from you guys giving me feedback, telling me that it was helpful, pointing out some alternatives or just making friends online, is something you can't replicate in the real world.
This happens more often lately that I do google less, but instead search my second brain as I have written it down as I googled it already more than once.
Never would I have thought, as an organized Swiss person, that I would leave the path of putting everything into folder structures to find easily.
Imagine if everyone would update their articles or notes instead of creating copies repeatedly; the internet would get a web of remarkable, highly valuable notes.
Think of it as SQLite for analytics workloads but with a fast columnar-vectorized query execution engine.
Loading CSVs and precisely correcting data types is still not a solved problem in 2024.
Think of it as the Swiss army knife of data engineers.
Simplify. Replace Apache Spark with DuckDB where possible. Spark is a complex setup, even more so for tuning and debugging.
As DuckDB can be embedded into data operations, all compute is done within the existing process.
Developers can move the data closer to the application or user, making the analytical experience magnitudes faster as you save the roundtrips from the client to the server.
Not many other databases can handle such a broad range of use cases, so it's hard to explain DuckDB to someone new.

Call it Infrastructure as Code, Visualization as Code, BI as Code, or anything as code. YAML engineering, or DSL—no matter what, it's the way the data stack evolved to empower non-programmers to create data pipelines and infrastructure without losing the benefits of SW engineering best practices through declarative methods.
A declarative data stack cleanly separates semantics from implementation, decoupling the business code from technical implementation, the how is rendered by the data stack engine.
As dbt doesn't know anything about its SQLs, it just runs them; it's limited to doing anything data-aware.

Also, if you are declarative, you can make the code work for a very long time. For example, the browser runs the code from Craigslist from 1995, and it's only possible to this day because HTML is declarative.
Think of Markdown. It is universally portable, and almost every application can render it with its various engines.
Once you have a declarative data stack, you essentially have your low-code or no-code platform, as you can build an API/UI on top of your configs.
At its core, GenBI delivers an unreasonably effective human interface, where we iterate quickly, based on BI-as-Code.

Self-service BI has been around for a while, trying to enable business users to create dashboards and reports. But that transition has always been hard, and without SQL or even Python skills to wrangle and clean your data, it's hard. I think GenBI is the next attempt for Self-Service BI, which is very promising.

While AI can control visual interfaces, this approach is inefficient for complex business logic. The data model joins, metric aggregations and bus matrix require explicit declarations that AI can parse and understand; print-screened images are insufficient.
Code-first enables a smaller data team to do more. Think of the Ruby on Rails developer, who does end-to-end from changing the database theme to the front of the app.
If we can further automate this process, we will overcome one of the biggest problems of BI: the bottleneck of the data or BI teams to ship or publish data artifacts.
This is usually where technical data professionals care least, so a trained model that helps us would significantly boost productivity.

Modern computers are powerful enough that many organizations can effectively run their analytics workloads on a single machine, making DuckDB an attractive option for those seeking a more straightforward, more maintainable data stack without sacrificing performance or features.

In six months, their defensive cyber operations team processed 7.5 trillion records across 130 million files using thousands of concurrent DuckDB instances, handling data spikes from 1.5 TB to 50 TB per day without infrastructure changes.
This approach dramatically reduced their data processing costs from $2,000/day with Snowflake while maintaining system robustness and security.
DuckDB's ability to efficiently handle Parquet files on S3 makes it particularly powerful for data lake architectures.

When writing queries in Python, DuckDB returns a 'relation' object (an abstract representation of the query) rather than immediately materializing the total result. This relation can be stored in a variable and used in subsequent queries, making SQL more composable.
Testing locally, 100 times a day, before going into production can save much money and speed up testing cycles.
The best way to understand is through hands-on experience. Import a CSV, wrangle some data, execute some queries, visualize a large local data set, or read distributed files from S3.
Wouldn't it be convenient to separate the presentation from the storage through a data modeling layer, similar to the model layer?
A place where we can declaratively define our metrics, measures, and business KPIs so that we can update them confidently and version them.
Today, we have the beginning of such a separation of concerns with a Semantic Layer.
This architectural pattern can solve similar challenges in data engineering that MVC solved for web development.
Modern data teams face growing complexity: multiple data sources, various BI tools, and ever-increasing self-service analytics.
This is where a semantic layer shines: It provides a single source of truth for business metrics while abstracting away the underlying complexity.
Organizations must maintain consistency across their metrics while making data accessible to business users.
Data-driven decision-making is crucial for business success, but organizations face a growing challenge of complexity and data governance.
These challenges make it difficult to access data in a unified way.
In this final piece of the series, we examine the integration of a semantic layer with artificial intelligence and why it might be the best place to start with GenAI.
Tools that lack these declarative code paths are difficult to automate, because code generation is almost impossible (imagine trying to auto-generate Tableau dashboards or Informatica pipelines).
Essentially, designing a compiler/makefile for the data stack declared from a single function or config file.
The business logic and technical logic are all mixed up. It's harder to read already with this simple example, but the more significant our project, the more unreadable such imperative code gets.
Everything a developer interacts with should be declarative, including infrastructure, runtime, type evolution, CDC logic, credentials, and schedules.
We developers shouldn't make imperative decisions about scope and execution; the engine should handle that.
When users fix an issue, they improve the engine's templates rather than modifying generated code, benefiting all future deployments.
Open standards form the foundation for openness and integration, decoupling the data stack, which is also crucial for creating a DDS engine.
To be proficient as a data engineer, you need to know various toolkits—from fundamental Linux commands to different virtual environments and optimizing efficiency as a data engineer.
Think of container packages on ships that transport goods; the breakthrough was the standardized container size that fits on every harbor could maneuver them.
You don't need to be an expert, but you shouldn't be afraid of command line tools and know some basic Linux commands.
The core responsibility of a data engineer is to orchestrate different jobs in the correct order and fully automate them.
In line with the Unix Philosophy, to make one tool do one thing as best as possible, you can combine 'pipe' different tools.
Instead of learning as many as possible, I suggest investing in a few used at your company and where you benefit most.
This foundation reminds us that sometimes the simplest solution is the most effective—a well-chosen Linux command can often replace a complex toolchain.
It's better to understand why than skip over it quickly.
A pivot table is a REPL for tables. You can drill and slice; you get direct feedback, and it's fast and instant.
Allowing analysts from fifteen minutes of complicated data table and database functions to 'just seconds' of dragging fields into place.
If we can't understand and can't control it, we also trust it less. Trust is a human instinct that goes far. Once the trust in your data is lost, it's nearly impossible to get it back.
The enduring power of pivot tables is their robustness, simple usage, and fast, interactive response. It's the Lingua Franca of data if you are not fluent in the language of SQL or Python.
Pivot is the OG of a dashboard. Without a pivot table, how would you create a dashboard?
Is it worth spending hours perfecting a dashboard's aesthetics when you really need to understand how your company is performing?
The secret to their longevity might lie in their intuition interface through reasonable constraints and standardization around measures and dimensions.

There is no talking block, only writer's block.
Unlimited freedom is super-blocking, especially for creativity.

I think of my creative process as gardening. Things inside us build up slowly, but great things will come out with constant nurturing.
When I don't have inspiration or I have a block, I do nothing. I live.
Besides the time/date dimension, geography is probably the second most used dimension.
With DuckDB, all your data preparation, integration, and analysis are consolidated into a single database. Spatial support is just an extension away.
Instead of 100 billion computations, spatial indexing may only need to look up 1M grid cells, each lookup comparing against nearby polygons (~10 instead of 100K).
GIS and maps are challenging, and there is a lot to cover.
Geospatial analysis powers countless daily applications—from delivery services to store locators—often invisibly enhancing our digital experiences.
With DuckDB, this analytical power becomes accessible to every data engineer, democratizing capabilities once reserved for GIS specialists.
Everything is hard. That's the thing with data visualization. You have to understand the data. You have to understand the context. You have to understand the technique.
Postgres is the Swiss Army knife of databases, and for many applications, it's more than sufficient.

Cloud data warehouses are built – like physical warehouses in the real world – to enable storage and retrieval at massive scales with efficiency. However, in order to achieve these efficiencies, they rely on an architecture that separates storage and compute, introducing seconds of incompressible latency for every query.
While the technical evolution of analytical systems appears to be a linear progression, it is cyclical, addressing the same fundamental challenge: balancing performance with flexibility.

Early OLAP systems achieved incredible speed through pre-aggregation but sacrificed flexibility. Modern systems return to OLAP principles with dynamic rather than static aggregation, enabled by hardware advances that early OLAP architects could only dream of.
The significant difference between these analytical databases is the trade-off between cost (dollars-per-TB-stored) and performance (seconds-per-TB-scanned).

At the end of the day, engineers need to choose an analytics database technology based on trade-offs across cost, performance, scale, and complexity—which ultimately only our business use cases can determine.
The key is to not repeat ourselves.

What began as an abstraction layer for LLM applications has, for many teams, become a redundant orchestration tool that sounds and feels a lot like an ETL tool or orchestrator that teams already have in place.
As someone with two decades working with data, this lesson stands out above all others—avoid creating parallel systems for what could be extensions of existing ones.
Instead of multiple specialized tools for AI workloads, the sustainable path forward integrates vector operations directly into existing data platforms.
Vector operations should complement, not replace, your well-established data engineering practices.
Data engineers already excel at building reliable pipelines, transforming data, and ensuring consistency—these skills transfer directly to AI workflows when the right integrations are in place.
By prioritizing integration over isolation, data engineers can harness the power of vector technologies while building cohesive, maintainable data platforms that stand the test of time.

The strategic decision to move data quality checks, business logic, transformations, and governance processes earlier in the data lifecycle—closer to the source or ingestion phase—rather than handling these concerns downstream in BI tools or at the point of consumption.

We don't need to invest massive effort in exporting large SQL or code from our BI tool, or worse, hugely generated code, and trying to convert that to our low-code ETL anymore. We can move descriptive business logic simply from one config file to another.
Errors identified and fixed earlier (more to the left) are less expensive and typically easier to debug. This was not possible with GUI-first approaches; code-first tools open up new possibilities.
Most data quality problems are code quality problems.
Data quality isn't a data team's problem; it's clearly defined best practices that everyone in the organization understands and can follow.
If 100% of issues were caught during development, data quality would never be a concern.
We save compute by moving code from the BI dashboard, which is executed every time a user clicks on the dashboard, to a pipeline where the query is pre-materialized and only paid for once.
The orchestrator is the heart of any data engineering project, potentially any data platform.

Data workloads are different. These are less time-critical and usually vastly complex with upstream data out of our control; we need to implement hack upon hack, and need to deliver high-quality data to the users.
Without a data orchestrator, you won't have a scheduler, data lineage, visualizer, or handy function to restart a process. You end up with disjointed stored procedures.

Coding is hard, especially for business specialists who need to keep up within their field and domain and just need to get their jobs scheduled. You can't expect them to learn programming on the side.

Generally, software best practices are hard to apply to data engineering. We know that from the history of SSIS and Informatica, GUI-driven tools where you need to click through 100 tables if you want to load tables.

Imagine an airplane; it's vastly complex. No person, not even a pilot, can know the inside out of every jet, every piece. That's why they created a cockpit, with hundreds of lights and knobs to turn and see what's going on.
Logs are the lifeblood of our data platforms
Data platforms are essentially built around logs. We can't debug the data stack; the logs are our way to find the error later on.
Software engineers can debug more easily, as they are in control of what the user can and can't do. But data is different, constantly changing and flowing from A to B.
As we can't simply use a debugger with complex data pipelines, we must log our way through problems.

DuckDB is the ultimate log parser. It can run with zero-copy, meaning you don't need to install or insert logs into DuckDB, but you can read from your data lake in S3, from your Snowflake Warehouse, and from your servers via HTTPS server, all within a single binary.
The better the logs, the less we need to search, and the more we can focus our time on fixing the bugs.
Knowing how to analyze logs straightforwardly and efficiently is a competitive advantage in today's data-driven world.
Imagine building enterprise data infrastructure where you write 90% less code but deliver twice the value.
A declarative data stack is a set of tools and, precisely, its configs can be thought of as a single function such as run_stack(serve(transform(ingest))) that can recreate the entire data stack.
Unlike Airflow, where each task has no idea what is happening inside of it, it's a black box.
The return on investment in the platform grows with rising complexity.
Breaking free from black boxes doesn't mean abandoning the open data stack, but rather integrating best-in-class tools under a unified, configuration-driven approach.
The declarative approach offers a sustainable path forward that balances enterprise requirements with development agility.
Data lake table formats essentially allow you to store all your data cost-effectively while reaping the benefits of a warehouse or database.
An ICE Stack shifts from where the data is stored (database/cloud data warehouse, Oracle, Teradata) to a data stack and data platform.
With the vendor data lock-in removed, any vendor offering data computation has the advantage that fewer data platforms get locked into being strictly a 'Snowflake, Databricks, BigQuery, or AWS shop'.
As the data itself doesn't move because it stays in open table formats, we can now easily choose and pick the best engine for every job.
While the managed Iceberg with all the additional services is most welcome, the proprietary API is not.

The true power of this open approach lies in its flexibility—data remains in open formats, engines become interchangeable, and organizations maintain ownership of their most valuable asset: their data.
This is a significant shift from earlier eras of enterprise data infrastructure, where customers chose and were locked into major vendors like SAP, Oracle, or even IBM Mainframes.
If people ask me how they should start writing or how to get a job, I always say to buy a domain first.
Every time they had a new domain. To me, that's so sad.

Tech Independence basically means that you do not depend on any particular company or software.
We always believe we just have to use what's out there to buy, but there are other ways.

Linux runs the world. There is almost no digital device that we use that is not running Linux or part of it.
You also don't get a heart attack if Google turns off your favorite app.
Open Table Format bundles distributed files into manageable tables with database-like features. Newer features enhance and facilitate access and data governance, similar to a lakehouse.
The compute layer is responsible for creating files on S3, creating an Iceberg table, or managing the request for the number of tables sent to the catalog via API.
Open Standards over Silos - instead of loading data into proprietary formats of cloud vendors and getting locked in, we work directly with open standards.
This tremendously simplifies the work we have to do on the data engineering side; we can avoid creating denormalization pipelines and data duplication solely for reporting purposes.
No matter how good GenAI or GenBI is, humans are still irreplaceable in interpreting the results and understanding the domain.
It also shows that the English language is not always precise enough. That's why SQL is sometimes better to use or to explain to an LLM, so we communicate exactly what we want.
We might even see a decline in dashboard use per se, as self-service analytics is now possible for the first time via chat with the analytics backend.
Moving from orchestration theory to the enterprise level is a real challenge. How do you handle secrets across environments? Where does your business logic actually live?

In the old days, we put it into stored procedures—custom code that databases had like PL/SQL in Oracle and T-SQL in Microsoft SQL Server. In the world of composable data stacks, the business logic is usually in SQL statements, Python, or YAML.
Think of it as a resource that data engineers build, which less technical people can reuse.

Why are code artifacts such as YAML the perfect setup for AI? Because AI has a very limited context window, but if you give it an intermediate stage, think of it like in-memory RAM that both the LLM and the human can work together on.

Data orchestration is becoming more accessible, but the underlying complexity hasn't disappeared. Tools like Kestra handle that complexity so teams can focus on what their data should accomplish rather than how to move it around.
Unlike many other orchestrators, you do not need to know how to code or have a technical background.
With the right setup, conversations can generate BI artifacts.
Domain experts know the business and numbers best, so they are also the best ones to check the numbers and iteratively create BI artifacts.
Many data warehouses or data lakes aren't fast enough to support conversation-speed interrogations.
By going narrower, like analyzing a spike in a dashboard, it's much less likely to hallucinate and makes its prediction more deterministic.
Conversational BI is self-serve, too, but it is slower and more imprecise in a grand context, though super helpful in a narrow context.
The key insight from our exploration of conversational BI is fundamental: conversations can generate code, and code generates insights.
Conversational BI finally delivers on the promise of self-serve BI that the industry has pursued for two decades.

With a semantic layer, your revenue KPI or other complex company measures are defined once in a single source of truthâno need to re-implement them over and over again.
The magic is that we can now change the metric in the YAML file, add a CASE WHEN statement, or fix a formatting error all without touching the query or code.

Try implementing a 'revenue per customer by quarter with year-over-year comparison' across five different BI tools using just database viewsâyou'll most probably end up with five different implementations that drift apart over time.
dataset â  aggregations, table columns â  metrics, physical table â  logical definition
The beauty of semantic layers lies in their ability to simply define dependencies on complex measures, eliminating the need to repeat 100 lines of SQL code in your CTE query.
Unlike pre-computed aggregation tables that need to be reprocessed when source data changes, semantic layers generate queries on demand.
Querying billions of weather records and getting results in under 200 milliseconds isn't theory; it's what real-time analytics solutions provide.
If we have the best tools but a bad data flow, it's not worth much.
The moment the data is loaded, it is outdated.
ClickHouse doesn't only serve as storage but can handle much of the transformation and aggregation work directly, eliminating traditional ETL bottlenecks.
The fundamental principle underlying all these approaches is minimizing joins at query time.
MergeTree engines are specifically designed for high data ingest rates and massive data volumes.
Data sketches use clever algorithms to deliver 99%+ accuracy for 1% of the cost and storage.
ClickHouse lets us handle powerful use cases without the need for expensive ETL pipelines or an additional semantic layer.

Companies often use multiple platforms simultaneously: Lovable for apps, OpenAI and Claude for AI, AWS and GCP for infrastructure. Tracking costs across these services becomes nearly impossible without a unified dashboard.

After I got started, I quickly noticed that wasn't going to be that easy. You need to set up a Cost export report within the portal of AWS, store the data in an S3 bucket, and then from there you can download the data in the appropriate format.
This project started with what seemed like a simple idea: pull costs from a few APIs, throw them in a dashboard, done. But cloud cost data doesn't work that way.
Everything is made composable, meaning you can add extra extraction with dlt or query and visualize additional data with Rill without spinning up infrastructure.
